title: Lasthenia leptalea
intro: Lasthenia leptalea, also known as the San Diego goldfields or coast range goldfields, is a small, annual wildflower native to the southwestern United States. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in relieving sore throats, skin irritations, and respiratory issues. Bioactive constituents of Lasthenia leptalea include sesquiterpene lactones, such as leptalenolide, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used in herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and salves, which are used to treat various health conditions and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Lasthenia leptalea is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the family Asteraceae. It is a member of the genus Lasthenia, which consists of about 20 species of annual and perennial plants native to North America. The family Asteraceae is commonly known as the sunflower or daisy family, and it comprises a wide range of plant species. The classification of Lasthenia leptalea reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants within the Asteraceae family. This classification system helps scientists understand the plant's characteristics and behaviors.
